nameMeanings.name: Rada
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Carlos, Delfin, Ernesto, Jose, Lilia, Adelardo, Alipio, Arquimedes, Arturo, Cesar, Cruz, Eladia.

Czech and Slovak: from a short form of any of various Slavic personal names containing the element rad ‘merry, glad’ (from Old Slavic radъ), such as Radoslav, in which the second element means ‘glory’ or ‘famous’.
Hungarian: from the old personal name Rada or Ráda, of Slavic origin (see 1 above and Rad ).

variants.name: Rach
meanings: German: from Middle High German rach ‘rough, stiff’, a nickname for someone affected by hard work, or a topographic name from the same word in the sense ‘steep, rough’.
Czech, Polish, and Sorbian: from a pet form of the personal names beginning with Ra-, especially Slavic names such as Czech Radoslav, Polish and Sorbian Radosław (see Rada ).

variants.name: Radach
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Christoph, Otto, Waltraut.
German: from a short form of any of various Slavic personal names based on Old Slavic radъ ‘merry, glad’ (see Rada 1).
Americanized form of Czech Radáč: from a pet form of a personal name containing the Slavic element rad ‘merry, glad’ (see Rada 1).

variants.name: Rad
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Arabic/Muslim Ali, Bijan, Abbas, Amad, Masoud, Nader, Nasser, Saeed, Ahmad, Alireza, Amir, Azin.

Iranian: from Persian rād ‘bold, brave’, also ‘skilful, learned’, forming the second part (originally a suffix) of compound names such as Mahdavirad or Mahdavi Rad (see Mahdavi ). The surname may also be from the personal name Rād, based on the same word. Compare Raad 2.
German: from a short form of an ancient Germanic personal name formed with rād, rāt ‘advice, counsel’.

variants.name: Radack
meanings: German: from a Germanized pet form of any of various Slavic personal names based on Old Slavic radъ ‘merry, glad’ (see Rada 1, compare Radak ).
Americanized form of Serbian and Croatian   Radak  .

History: The progenitor of many of the American Radacks was Ephraim Radack, a German from Alt Danzig in the Russian Empire (today Karlivka in Ukraine), a settlement founded by the colonists from Danzig in Prussia (today Gdansk in Poland). He and his family immigrated to SD in the 1870s.

nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Beaumont
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Gilles, Gaetan, Honore, Jacques, Jean-Guy, Marcel.

English (of Norman origin) and French: habitational name from any of the five places in Normandy or several others elsewhere in France so named. The placename comes from Old French beu, bel ‘fair, lovely’ + mont ‘hill’. There are also places in England so named under Norman influence in Cumberland, Lancashire, and Essex, the last of which changed its name in the 12th century from Fulepet ‘foul pit’ to Bealmont ‘beautiful hill’; these may also have given rise to the surname. The surname is now widespread throughout England, but most common in Yorkshire. Compare Belmont .

History: Many English bearers of this surname are descended from Henry de Beaumont (died 1340), who came to England from France as a soldier, serving Kings Edward I, II, and III of England. He acquired the earldom of Buchan in Scotland by marriage. — Many American bearers of this surname are descendants of John Beaumont (1612–47), who came to North America from England in 1630. William Beaumont came from England to Cambridge MA in 1635 and subsequently moved to CT.

variants.name: Bement
meanings: English (Cambridgeshire): variant of   Beaumont  . This surname is now rare in Britain.

variants.name: Beeman
meanings: English (Kent): occupational name for a beekeeper, from Middle English bee-man ‘bee-keeper’ (Old English bēo + mann).
English (Kent): variant of   Beaumont  .
Americanized form of German Biemann or of its Dutch cognate Bieman.

variants.name: Belmont
meanings: French and English (of Norman origin): variant of   Beaumont  .
Catalan: habitational name from the placename Bellmont ‘beautiful mountain’. Compare Spanish   Belmonte  .

variants.name: Baumunk
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Siegmund.
German: probably a Germanized form of a French Huguenot name Beaumont (see also Beaumont ).

variants.name: Bayman
meanings: English (mainly Lancashire): variant of   Beaumont  .
Americanized form of German Beumann or Bäumann, variants of Baumann .

nameMeanings.name: Teresa
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: form of   Theresa .  In the English-speaking world the name is often chosen in this spelling by Roman Catholics, with particular reference to the Spanish saint, Teresa of Ávila (  Teresa Cepeda de Ahumada ,  1515–82 ).

variants.name: Theresa
meanings: Of problematic origin. The name seems to have been first used in Spain and Portugal, and, according to tradition, was the name of the wife of St Paulinus of Nola, who spent most of his life in Spain; she was said to have originated (and to have derived her name) from the Greek island of Thēra. However, this story is neither factually nor etymologically confirmed.

variants.name: Teresia
meanings: Of problematic origin. The name seems to have been first used in Spain and Portugal, and, according to tradition, was the name of the wife of St Paulinus of Nola, who spent most of his life in Spain; she was said to have originated (and to have derived her name) from the Greek island of Thēra. However, this story is neither factually nor etymologically confirmed.

en.lifeSummary: When Teresa Rada De Beaumont was born in 1203, in Rada, Voto, Cantabria, Spain, her father, Gil de Rada, was 10 and her mother, Teresa De Beaumont, was 13. She married Ortún Ortíz de Zúñiga Fernandez in 1224, in Zúñiga, Zuñiga, Nafarroa, Spain. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 2 daughters.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Teresa Rada De Beaumont est née en 1203, à Rada, Voto, Cantabria, Espagne, son père, Gil de Rada, avait 10 ans et sa mère, Teresa De Beaumont, avait 13 ans. Elle a épousé Ortún Ortíz de Zúñiga Fernandez en 1224, à Zúñiga, Zuñiga, Nafarroa, Espagne. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 2 filles.

ko.lifeSummary: Teresa Rada De Beaumont(이)가 1203년 스페인 캔타브리아 보토 라다에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Gil de Rada(은)는 10세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Teresa De Beaumont(은)는 13세였다. 그녀는 1224년 스페인 나파로 주n익아 즈un익아에서 Ortún Ortíz de Zúñiga Fernandez(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Teresa Rada De Beaumont родилась в 1203 г. в местности рада, фото, кантабря, Испания, её отцу по имени Gil de Rada было 10 лет, а матери, которую звали Teresa De Beaumont, 13. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени Ortún Ortíz de Zúñiga Fernandez в 1224 г. в местности зunига, зуnига, нафарроа, Испания. У них было по крайней мере 2 сына и 2 дочери.
